text:
After a week of waiting, track cycling is finally taking centre stage at theParis2024Olympics, with the keirin, omnium and madison particular highlights of the schedule.

Team GB are hoping to improve on their slightly underwhelming tally of three medals collected in the velodrome three years ago in Tokyo, even if that was enough to see Great Britain finish top of the track cycling medal table once again.

Follow Metro.co.uk’s live blog for updates from the Paris 2024 Olympics

So, how exactly do these showpiece events work?Metro.co.ukis on hand to talk you through all the details.

The keirin is one of the most traditional events in track cycling.

A total of 30 riders take part in the keirin, grouped into five heats of six riders, with the top two automatically progressing to the quarter-finals, 12 advancing to the semi-finals and six competing in the medal race.

Each round involves riders beginning the race withthree laps behind the Derny bike, which gradually reaches a speed of 50km per hour, before departing the track with three laps to go, from which point it is a straight race to the finish line to determine who qualifies for the next round.

The omnium is held over one day and features four events: the scratch race, the tempo race, the elimination race and the points race.

Points are awarded for the first three events based on a points scale, from 40 points for first, 38 for second and so on, which are then carried over to the points race which is a series of sprints.

Riders gain and lose points from their total during the points race, with the winner the one with the most points at the end.

The scratch race is a straightforward race, with points awarded based on where riders finish over a distance of 10km for men and 7.5km for women, while the tempo race is contested over the same distances but points are awarded to the rider crossing the line first on each lap. A further 20 points are awarded to any rider who laps the field.

The elimination race, meanwhile, sees the last rider cross the line after every two laps eliminated.

The madison consists of two members per team and is essentially a relay race, with the riders switching between racing and resting depending on who is the lead rider of the two on the track.

The men’s madison takes place over 200 laps (around 31 miles), whilst the women’s madison is contested over 120 laps (around 18.5 miles), with medals awarded based on points accumulation.

Sprints take place every 10 laps and offer five points for first, three points for second, two points for third and one point for fourth.

Any team which laps another team earns an additional 20 points, with 20 points deducted for competitors who are lapped. Double points are on offer in the last sprint, starting from a maximum of ten.

The Madison was not part of the Olympic schedule at the London and Rio games, making its return at Tokyo 2020, which was also the first time ever that women competed in the Madison.

title: What are the keirin, omnium and madison at the Olympics? | Metro News
description: Find out what the keirin, omnium and madison are at the Paris 2024 Olympics and the rules as to how the Track Cycling events work.
